story:
Lost. Confused. Scared. Discombobulated. Especially discombobulated. I’m terrified Captain Arrow will turn out like Jack. Why did that two‑legged pirate invade my world? I was fine before he showed. Happy even. Now?

I try sewing to distract myself and stab my fingers three times. The needle is Jack—small stings that remind me of bigger wounds. I remember the first slap. Mild compared to the rest. Some scars still live on my skin. I shake him off, grab my coat, and head to my office. Work helps.

The night in Jorton is crisp and alive—watch towers glowing, candles in windows, street torches fanning gold over stone. Dwarfs, men, and stumpers huddle around campfires. Magic hangs in the cold air. How did this town not exist a few years ago?

Oakaford, Captain of the Stumper Guard, steps out of the shadows. “A fine night for a walk,” he says. We talk of stars, and he remembers the feeling of leaves falling, how it made him lighter. He tilts his wooden head and hints about love. I dodge. He bows, apologizes with his mouth, and laughs with his eyes.

In my office, I review contingency plans. Shadow elves. Their trunkers. If war comes, we must be ready. Bigger concerns than Arrow. After an hour, sleepiness finally settles in. I snuff the candles and head home.

By Ye Old Guard, a suave figure leans against a torchpole. Only one man in Jorton is that smooth. “My Lady,” Arrow says with a bow and a grin. I tease that he’s hallucinating. He likes this hallucination. His eyes dance in the firelight. Dashing. So was Jack, once. My face betrays me.

“Did I say something wrong?” he asks.

“Do you know what people are saying about us?” I shoot back. Love, apparently. We both test the wind, act casual, declare it untrue—“on the same scroll,” we say, too carefully. He wishes me good night. In bed, sleep abandons me. Was he disappointed? Does he love me? Do I love him? I chase those questions until I finally conk out.

Morning arrives on a tide of Mom’s loaded eggs. Dad and I wait as patiently as wolves. He asks about my late stroll. Shadow elves? Both, I admit. Mom looks too long—using her magic to read the room inside me. I laugh it off and try to hide Jack, but my parents know. Mom slices through my defenses with the gentlest blade: rumors of budding love. I try my duchess voice. They snort. I crack. “What if Arrow turns out to be like Jack?”

Silence. Then Mom: “Do you really believe that? Or is Jack still beating you by making you afraid to love?” It lands like truth. Jack is rotting in prison, but he’s still stealing from me if I let fear run my life. I remember who found me, who warned me, who saved me. My parents have my back. If they believe Arrow is good, maybe I can risk believing it, too.

I square my shoulders. I won’t let Jack win. Arrow and I are dating. Gossipers can tend their own fires and leave mine alone.

That night, I try to light a campfire. Smoke. No flame. Arrow appears and offers help. I tell him I need to start this fire myself. He nods, says he hopes there’s a fire he can help with someday. I meet his eyes. He understands. I smile inside. “Perhaps.”
